does: This paper develops Par-S²ZPO, a federated reinforcement learning from human feedback algorithm designed for resource-constrained edge devices. The method combines zeroth-order optimization with binary perturbation to reduce communication, computation, and memory requirements across the federated setting. The algorithm partitions parameters across agents and uses sign-based stochastic perturbations to estimate policy gradients without explicit gradient computation. Theoretical analysis demonstrates convergence rates matching centralized RLHF in sample complexity while achieving faster policy update iterations, and experiments on MuJoCo tasks show improvements over federated averaging baselines.
connects: This work bridges zeroth-order optimization, federated learning, and reinforcement learning from human feedback—three increasingly important areas in distributed ML. It extends prior federated optimization research that addressed communication-efficient training through methods like FedAvg and gradient compression, while adopting zeroth-order techniques originally developed for gradient-free optimization in non-convex settings. The paper responds to the challenge of scaling RLHF, which typically requires substantial computational resources, to decentralized settings with heterogeneous agents. It builds on recent advances in sign-based and binary communication methods for federated learning, applying these ideas to the policy optimization phase of RLHF rather than supervised learning.
enables: This work opens pathways for deploying large language model alignment techniques on distributed, resource-limited hardware without centralized training. It suggests that zeroth-order methods could become standard for federated RLHF, potentially enabling privacy-preserving alignment of language models across edge devices and organizations. The efficiency gains in memory and communication create possibilities for on-device preference learning and personalized model fine-tuning. Future directions include extending the approach to more complex RLHF settings with variable feedback mechanisms, combining these techniques with parameter-efficient methods like LoRA for federated LLM alignment, and exploring whether the zeroth-order framework generalizes to other federated deep RL tasks beyond continuous control.
